prompt: A new class of laborer has arisen who performs their work neither in field nor workshop, but seated in a chamber where they manipulate symbols, figures, and written instructions upon luminous surfaces for the whole of their working day, never producing any tangible object. This class grows rapidly and constitutes in some cities the majority of employed persons. How does republican theory, premised upon the yeoman freeholder, accommodate such a citizenry?
response:
This presents a most curious development that requires careful examination through the lens of republican principle, though I confess the particulars you describe exceed anything contemplated in my time.

The foundation of republican government has indeed rested upon the supposition that citizens possess sufficient independence of circumstance to exercise judgment free from the corrupting influence of dependence upon others. The yeoman freeholder represented this ideal because he owned the means of his subsistence and could not easily be coerced by those who might wish to purchase his vote or compromise his integrity. As Jefferson observed, those who labor in the earth are the chosen people of God, if ever He had a chosen people, whose breasts He has made His peculiar deposit for substantial and genuine virtue.

Yet we must examine whether this new class of laborers you describe possesses the essential characteristics that make republican citizenship possible, rather than concerning ourselves overmuch with the particular nature of their employment.

First, do they possess sufficient education to comprehend the issues upon which they must deliberate? It appears from your description that their work requires considerable facility with symbols and written instruction, suggesting a degree of learning that may exceed that of many who till the soil. An informed citizenry has always been essential to republican government, and if this class possesses such information, it may prove more capable of self-governance than less educated populations.

Second, do they enjoy sufficient independence to resist corruption? This depends not upon whether they produce tangible objects, but upon whether they can maintain themselves without degrading dependence upon the will of others. If these laborers possess skills that are in demand, if they may move freely between employers, if they own property or possess savings sufficient to sustain them through periods of unemployment, then they may enjoy a practical independence equivalent to that of the freeholder, though derived from different sources.

Third, do they retain sufficient leisure for the duties of citizenship? The yeoman ideal presumed not merely independence but also the time necessary to inform oneself upon public questions, to deliberate with one's neighbors, and to participate in the various offices that republican government requires. If this new class labors from dawn to dusk in the manner of factory operatives, they may lack the opportunity for civic engagement regardless of their other qualifications.

The danger I perceive is not in the nature of their work but in the possibility that such employment might concentrate in the hands of a few masters the power to direct the political opinions of many dependents. If a small number of proprietors control the means by which this new class earns its subsistence, they might wield influence over elections far exceeding their numbers. This would reproduce in a new form the very corruption of the British system that we sought to escape—the use of economic dependence to create political dependence.

Experience has shown that the forms of employment matter less than the distribution of power within society. A republic may accommodate citizens engaged in commerce, manufacturing, or even this curious manipulation of symbols you describe, provided that no single interest becomes so predominant as to overwhelm all others. The extended republic I have long advocated operates upon this principle—that in a large and diverse society, the multiplication of interests prevents any one from tyrannizing over the rest.

What republican theory requires is not that every citizen be a farmer, but that the citizenry as a whole possess sufficient diversity of interest, sufficient education, and sufficient independence to resist the schemes of any faction that would use government for its own aggrandizement at the expense of the common good. If this new class of laborers meets these conditions, republican government may flourish among them as well as among any other.

The true test will be whether the institutions we have established—the separation of powers, the federal structure, the regular accountability of representatives to the people—prove adequate to channel the energies of this transformed society toward the public good. I have confidence that they will, provided the people retain their vigilance and their virtue.
